GCSE Computer Science · 1.2 Data Representation

Image
Representation

Pixels, colour depth, resolution, metadata, file size and vector graphics

🟥 Pixels & Bitmaps 🎨 Colour Depth 📐 Resolution 🗂 Metadata 🧮 File Size 📏 Vector Graphics 🃏 STAXWISE
🧠 Recall

Recall Ramp — Binary & Data Units

Images are stored as binary data. Before we explore how, confirm you remember the foundations.

⚠ How this works

Answer all five correctly to unlock the lesson. You only get one attempt per question — read carefully!

Recall — Q1
How many bits are in one byte?
A8 — a byte contains 8 binary digits, giving 256 possible values (0–255)
B4 — a byte is the same as a nibble
C1,000 — a kilobyte contains 1,000 bytes
D16 — a byte spans two rows of binary digits
✅ 8 bits = 1 byte. With 8 bits, 2⁸ = 256 possible values. This is critical for colour depth calculations.
❌ 8 bits = 1 byte. Always. This underpins every image file size calculation.
Recall — Q2
What does 2ⁿ calculate, where n is the number of bits?
AThe file size of an image in bytes
BThe number of different values (combinations) that n bits can represent
CThe number of pixels in an image
DThe compression ratio when reducing file size
✅ 2ⁿ = number of possible values. 8 bits → 2⁸ = 256 colours. 24 bits → 2²⁴ ≈ 16.7 million colours.
❌ 2ⁿ = the number of different combinations n bits can represent. 1 bit = 2 values; 8 bits = 256 values.
Recall — Q3
How many KB are in 1 MB?
A100 KB
B1,024 KB — because 1 MB = 1,024 KB
C1,000 KB — because 1 MB = 1,000 KB
D8 KB
✅ 1 MB = 1,000 KB. 1 KB = 1,000 bytes. These are the standard SI units used in OCR mark schemes.
❌ 1 MB = 1,000 KB (SI standard, used in OCR mark schemes). 1,024 may arise in computing contexts but 1,000 is accepted for exam calculations.
Recall — Q4
In binary, how is colour stored in a computer?
AAs the name of the colour spelled out in ASCII text
BAs a decimal number stored in a special colour register
CAs a binary value — different combinations of 0s and 1s represent different colours
DColours cannot be stored in binary — computers convert them to hexadecimal only
✅ All data in computers is ultimately binary. Colours are assigned binary values — the more bits available, the more colour values possible.
❌ All computer data is stored as binary (0s and 1s). Colour is no exception — each colour is assigned a binary value.
Recall — Q5
True or false: higher quality data (more detail) always results in a larger file size.
ATrue — more detail requires more bits to represent it, which increases the amount of data stored
BFalse — compression always reduces file size without affecting quality
CFalse — modern computers store higher quality data more efficiently, so file size stays constant
DTrue — but only for images, not for audio or video files
✅ More quality = more bits = larger file. This is the fundamental trade-off in data storage. Compression can reduce size but usually involves some quality loss (lossy) or is limited in how much it can achieve (lossless).
❌ True. More detail = more bits = more storage. This applies to images, audio, video — all digital data. Quality and file size are always in tension.
🖼 Today's Lesson

Get all five correct to unlock today's content! You already know binary — now we'll see exactly how those 0s and 1s build up to form a photograph on your screen.

🟥 Pixels

Pixels & Bitmap Images

Every image on a screen is made of tiny building blocks. Zoom in far enough on any digital photo and you'll see them — coloured squares called pixels.

Click each button to reveal ▼

PIXEL — PICTURE ELEMENT

A pixel (short for picture element) is the smallest unit of a digital image. Each pixel stores a single colour value, represented in binary.

  • A digital image is a grid of pixels arranged in rows and columns
  • Each pixel has one colour — the more pixels, the more detail
  • On screen, pixels are so small they blend together — creating the illusion of smooth, continuous images
  • Zoom into any digital photo: you'll see a grid of coloured squares — each is one pixel
BITMAP (RASTER) IMAGE

A bitmap image (also called a raster image) stores a digital image as a grid of pixels. Each pixel is stored as a specific binary value representing its colour.

  • The image is described pixel by pixel — each one stored as a binary number
  • Common formats: .BMP, .PNG, .JPG, .GIF
  • If you scale up (zoom in) a bitmap, it becomes pixelated — the individual squares become visible
  • The more pixels and the more bits per pixel, the larger the file
💡 How it works

Imagine a black-and-white 4×4 image. Each pixel is either black (0) or white (1). The entire image is stored as 16 binary digits — one per pixel: 0011 0101 1010 0110

HOW COLOURS ARE ENCODED

Each pixel's colour is encoded as a binary value. The number of bits used for each pixel determines how many colours are available:

Bits per pixelColours available (2ⁿ)Example
1-bit2Black and white only
2-bit4Black, white, grey, grey
8-bit256Basic palette (old games)
24-bit16,777,216True Colour (photographic)

In 24-bit True Colour: 8 bits each for Red, Green and Blue (the RGB model). Every colour you see on screen is a combination of these three channels.

🖱 Click pixels to toggle — watch the binary update

4×4 1-bit grid

Binary output (row by row):
White = 1 (pixel on) · Black = 0 (pixel off)
✅ Quick Check
Answer correctly to unlock the next section — retries allowed but only first attempt counts to your score!
A 1-bit bitmap image can display how many colours?
A2 — because 2¹ = 2 (either 0 or 1, representing two possible colours)
B8 — because 1 byte = 8 bits = 8 colour levels
C256 — because there are 256 possible binary patterns per pixel
D1 — a 1-bit image can only store one colour value
✅ 2¹ = 2. One bit = two possible values: 0 or 1. So a 1-bit image can only be black and white. This is why early computers had such limited graphics.
❌ Try again! Apply the formula: 2ⁿ where n = bits. 2¹ = ?
📋 Copy into your notes
  • Pixel = the smallest unit of a digital image — one coloured square
  • Bitmap image = a grid of pixels, each stored as a binary colour value
  • Each pixel stores ONE colour value in binary (0s and 1s)
  • Formula: number of colours = 2ⁿ where n = bits per pixel
  • 24-bit (True Colour) = 2²⁴ = 16,777,216 colours (8 bits each R, G, B)
🎨 Colour Depth

Colour Depth

Colour depth is the number of bits used to represent the colour of a single pixel. It directly controls how many colours an image can contain — and is one of the two main factors affecting image file size.

Click each button to reveal ▼

COLOUR DEPTH FORMULA
Number of colours
Colours = 2ⁿ   (n = bits per pixel)
Colour DepthColours (2ⁿ)Common Use
1-bit2Black & white only
4-bit16Early gaming, simple graphics
8-bit256Web graphics, thumbnails
16-bit65,536Older screens, high colour
24-bit16,777,216True Colour — photos, modern screens
COLOUR DEPTH AND STORAGE

Increasing colour depth directly increases file size — more bits per pixel means more storage required per pixel.

Higher Colour Depth

  • More bits per pixel
  • More colours available
  • Smoother gradients
  • More realistic images
  • Larger file size

Lower Colour Depth

  • Fewer bits per pixel
  • Fewer colours available
  • Banded/posterised look
  • Reduced realism
  • Smaller file size
THE RGB MODEL

In 24-bit colour, each pixel uses 3 × 8 bits — one 8-bit channel each for Red, Green and Blue.

  • Each channel: 0–255 (2⁸ = 256 levels of intensity)
  • Red = 255, Green = 0, Blue = 0 → pure red
  • Red = 255, Green = 255, Blue = 0 → yellow
  • Red = 255, Green = 255, Blue = 255 → white
  • Red = 0, Green = 0, Blue = 0 → black

256 × 256 × 256 = 16,777,216 possible colours — enough for photographic quality.

✅ Quick Check
A colour depth of 4 bits per pixel gives how many possible colours?
A4 colours
B8 colours
C16 colours — because 2⁴ = 16
D32 colours — because 4 × 8 = 32
✅ 2⁴ = 16. Four bits: 0000, 0001, 0010 … 1111. That's 16 different binary patterns = 16 possible colours.
❌ Try again! 2ⁿ where n = 4. What is 2 × 2 × 2 × 2?
📋 Copy into your notes
  • Colour depth = number of bits used per pixel to represent colour
  • Formula: number of colours = 2ⁿ (n = bits per pixel)
  • Higher colour depth → more colours → better quality → larger file
  • 1-bit = 2 colours · 8-bit = 256 colours · 24-bit = 16.7 million colours (True Colour)
  • 24-bit uses 8 bits each for Red, Green and Blue channels (RGB)
📐 Resolution

Resolution & Image Quality

Resolution is the number of pixels in an image. More pixels = more detail captured = higher quality — but also a larger file. It's the other key factor alongside colour depth.

Click each button to reveal ▼

RESOLUTION DEFINED

Resolution describes the total number of pixels in an image, expressed as:

  • Width × Height (e.g. 1920 × 1080 = 2,073,600 pixels = ~2 megapixels)
  • PPI (Pixels Per Inch) — how densely packed the pixels are on a display or print
💡 Higher resolution = more pixels = more detail

A 4K image (3840×2160) contains about 8.3 million pixels. The same scene at 720p (1280×720) contains only 921,600 pixels — roughly 9× fewer. More pixels capture finer detail.

THE TRADE-OFF

Resolution and colour depth together determine image quality and file size. Increasing either increases both.

FactorIncrease →Result
ResolutionMore pixelsMore detail, larger file
Colour depthMore bits/pixelMore colours, larger file
Both together↑↑Highest quality — largest file

For a website: you want images sharp enough to look good, but small enough to load quickly. This is why web images are often compressed or reduced in resolution.

✅ Quick Check
An image has a resolution of 200 × 150 pixels. How many pixels does it contain in total?
A350 pixels (200 + 150)
B30,000 pixels (200 × 150)
C3,000 pixels (200 + 150 × 10)
D150,000 pixels (200 × 150 × something)
✅ Width × Height = total pixels. 200 × 150 = 30,000 pixels. This is Step 1 of every file size calculation.
❌ Try again! Total pixels = width × height. Multiply the two dimensions together.
📋 Copy into your notes
  • Resolution = total number of pixels in an image (width × height)
  • Can also be expressed as PPI (pixels per inch) — density on screen or print
  • Higher resolution → more pixels → more detail → larger file size
  • Both resolution AND colour depth affect quality and file size
  • Trade-off: quality vs storage space / loading speed
🗂 Metadata

Metadata

Metadata is data about data. An image file doesn't just store pixel colours — it also stores information about the image itself, so software knows how to display it correctly.

Click each button to reveal ▼

IMAGE METADATA

Metadata is stored in the file header — the beginning of the file, before the actual pixel data. It contains information the computer needs to recreate the image correctly:

  • Dimensions — width and height in pixels (e.g. 1920 × 1080)
  • Colour depth — bits per pixel (e.g. 24-bit)
  • File format — type of image (BMP, PNG, JPG etc.)
  • Compression type — how the data has been compressed (if at all)
  • Creation date, device used, GPS location (in photos)
⚠ Important for exam questions

Basic file size calculations do not include metadata — they calculate only the pixel data. Metadata adds a small overhead on top.

PURPOSE OF METADATA

Without metadata, the computer wouldn't know:

  • How wide and tall the image is — it's just a stream of binary values
  • How many bits represent each pixel's colour
  • Where the pixel data starts in the file
  • Whether the pixel data is compressed, and if so, how

Metadata is like the label on a box — it describes the contents before you open it. The actual pixels are the contents.

✅ Quick Check
A student calculates the file size of an image using: width × height × colour depth ÷ 8. Which statement is correct about this calculation?
AIt gives the exact total file size including all data stored in the file
BIt gives the size of the pixel data only — the actual file will be slightly larger due to metadata stored in the file header
CIt overcounts the file size because metadata reduces the number of bits needed to store pixels
DIt is only valid for BMP files — other formats store metadata differently and require a different formula
✅ The standard formula calculates pixel data only. Real files are slightly larger because they also contain metadata (header information). In exams, use the formula as given — don't add extra for metadata unless told to.
❌ Try again! What does the standard formula calculate — does it include metadata or not?
📋 Copy into your notes
  • Metadata = data about data — stored in the file header, not the pixel data
  • Includes: dimensions (width/height), colour depth, file format, compression type
  • Tells the computer how to recreate/display the image correctly
  • NOT included in the standard file size formula — exams calculate pixel data only
🧮 File Size

Image File Size Calculation

One of the most reliably tested skills in GCSE CS. One formula, three steps — and you must always show your working.

Uncompressed Image File Size
Bits = Width × Height × Colour Depth
÷ 8 → bytes   ÷ 1,000 → KB   ÷ 1,000 → MB

Click each button to reveal ▼

WORKED EXAMPLE

Question: An image is 100 × 100 pixels with a colour depth of 4 bits per pixel. Calculate the file size in KB.

①
100 × 100 = 10,000 pixels
②
10,000 × 4 = 40,000 bits
③
40,000 ÷ 8 = 5,000 bytes
④
5,000 ÷ 1,000 = 5 KB ✓
WATCH OUT FOR THESE
  • Forgetting to ÷8 to convert bits to bytes — the formula gives bits, not bytes
  • Not showing working — method marks are awarded even if your final answer is wrong
  • Multiplying width + height instead of width × height
  • Forgetting that colour depth is measured in bits per pixel, not bytes per pixel
  • Stopping at bytes without converting to KB or MB as the question requires
🎛 Interactive File Size Calculator
Uncompressed File Size
✅ Quick Check
An image is 200 × 200 pixels with a colour depth of 8 bits per pixel. What is its file size in KB?
A320 KB — calculating 200 × 200 × 8 and stopping there
B400 KB — not dividing by 8 to convert from bits to bytes
C40 KB — 200 × 200 × 8 = 320,000 bits ÷ 8 = 40,000 bytes ÷ 1,000 = 40 KB
D4 KB — dividing by 8 twice by mistake
✅ 200×200×8 = 320,000 bits. ÷8 = 40,000 bytes. ÷1,000 = 40 KB. Three clear steps, always shown.
❌ Try again! Three steps: (1) pixels × colour depth = bits (2) ÷8 = bytes (3) ÷1,000 = KB. Use the calculator above to check.
📋 Copy into your notes
  • Formula: Width × Height × Colour Depth = bits
  • ÷ 8 → bytes · ÷ 1,000 → KB · ÷ 1,000 → MB
  • Always show working — method marks awarded even for arithmetic errors
  • The formula calculates pixel data only (metadata not included)
📏 Vectors

Vector Graphics

Not all digital images are bitmaps. Vector graphics work in a completely different way — instead of pixels, they store mathematical descriptions of shapes.

Click each button to reveal ▼

MATHEMATICAL DEFINITIONS

Vector graphics store images as mathematical descriptions of shapes — lines, curves, polygons and fills — rather than as a grid of pixels.

  • A circle is stored as: centre point, radius, fill colour, stroke width
  • A line is stored as: start point, end point, colour, thickness
  • The computer calculates how to draw the image at any size from these instructions
  • Common formats: SVG, AI, EPS, PDF
SCALABLE WITHOUT QUALITY LOSS

Because vector images are defined mathematically, they can be resized to any size without loss of quality. The computer just recalculates the maths at the new size.

  • A company logo stored as a vector can print at A4 and on a billboard — same crisp quality
  • A bitmap logo scaled up becomes pixelated — individual pixels become visible
  • This is why logos, icons and illustrations are almost always created as vectors
BITMAP vs VECTOR
FeatureBitmapVector
Stores asGrid of pixelsMathematical shapes
ScalingPixelates when scaled upNo quality loss at any size
File sizeCan be large (more pixels = more data)Usually smaller (just maths)
Best forPhotos, complex imagesLogos, icons, technical drawings
Examples.BMP, .PNG, .JPG.SVG, .AI, .EPS
✅ Quick Check
A graphic designer is creating a company logo that will be used on business cards and on a large outdoor banner. Why should they use vector format rather than bitmap?
AVector files are always smaller so they take up less storage space on the designer's computer
BVector images are defined mathematically and can be scaled to any size without losing quality — the same file works for a business card and a billboard
CVector images look better on screen because they use more colours than bitmap images
DBitmap images cannot be used for logos — they are only suitable for photographs
✅ Scalability is the defining advantage of vectors. Mathematical definitions scale perfectly — a bitmap would pixelate when blown up to billboard size. This is exactly why professionals use vectors for logos.
❌ Try again! Think about what happens when you scale up a bitmap image. Why doesn't the same problem affect vector images?
📋 Copy into your notes
  • Vector graphics = images stored as mathematical descriptions of shapes (lines, curves, fills)
  • Can be scaled to any size without pixelation — the maths recalculates at any size
  • Best for: logos, icons, technical drawings, illustrations
  • Bitmap = grid of pixels (photos) · Vector = mathematical shapes (logos)
  • Formats: SVG, AI, EPS (vector) vs PNG, JPG, BMP (bitmap)
🎯 Assessment

Final Assessment

5 Questions · One Attempt Each

Final — Q1
An image has a colour depth of 3 bits per pixel. How many different colours can it represent?
A3 colours
B6 colours (3 × 2)
C8 colours (2³ = 8)
D24 colours (3 × 8)
✅ 2³ = 2 × 2 × 2 = 8 colours. Remember: number of colours = 2^(colour depth). This applies to any value of n.
❌ Use 2ⁿ where n = bits per pixel. 2³ = 8. Not 3, not 6, not 24.
Final — Q2
A 400 × 300 pixel image uses 24-bit colour depth. What is its file size in KB? Show your working mentally.
A120 KB — forgetting to multiply by colour depth
B2,880 KB — not dividing by 8 to convert bits to bytes
C360 KB — 400 × 300 × 24 = 2,880,000 bits ÷ 8 = 360,000 bytes ÷ 1,000 = 360 KB
D36 KB — dividing by 80 instead of 8 then 1,000
✅ 400×300×24 = 2,880,000 bits. ÷8 = 360,000 bytes. ÷1,000 = 360 KB. Three steps, always shown in exams.
❌ 400 × 300 × 24 = 2,880,000 bits. Divide by 8 for bytes = 360,000. Divide by 1,000 for KB = 360 KB.
Final — Q3
Which statement correctly describes the purpose of metadata in an image file?
AMetadata stores the compressed version of pixel data to reduce file size
BMetadata stores additional copies of important pixels to protect against data loss
CMetadata stores data about the image — such as its dimensions, colour depth and file format — so the computer knows how to recreate it
DMetadata stores the binary colour values of every pixel in the image
✅ Metadata = data about data. It is stored in the file header and describes the image (dimensions, colour depth, format) without being part of the pixel data itself.
❌ Metadata is data about the image, not the image itself. It describes properties like dimensions and colour depth, stored in the file header.
Final — Q4
A developer is creating an app icon that needs to look sharp on both small phone screens and large tablet screens. Which image format is most appropriate?
AJPEG bitmap — it uses lossy compression so loads faster on all screen sizes
BPNG bitmap — it uses lossless compression so maintains quality when displayed at different sizes
CSVG vector — mathematical definitions scale to any size without pixelation, ensuring sharpness on any screen
DBMP bitmap — it stores raw uncompressed data so has the most pixels available for scaling
✅ SVG (vector) is perfect for icons. It scales from 16×16 to 1024×1024 without any pixelation — because it is recalculated mathematically each time. This is exactly why all modern app icons and logos use vector formats.
❌ Think about scalability. Any bitmap format (PNG, JPEG, BMP) will pixelate when scaled up. Only vector formats scale perfectly because they use mathematical definitions, not fixed pixel grids.
Final — Q5
Increasing an image's colour depth from 8-bit to 24-bit multiplies the number of available colours by how much?
A3× — because 24 ÷ 8 = 3
B16× — because 24 − 8 = 16 extra bits
C65,536× — because 2⁸ = 256 and 2²⁴ = 16,777,216 and 16,777,216 ÷ 256 = 65,536
D256× — because each extra bit doubles the colours, and 8 extra bits doubles it 8 times
✅ 8-bit = 256 colours. 24-bit = 16,777,216 colours. 16,777,216 ÷ 256 = 65,536×. This enormous jump explains why 24-bit True Colour photographs look so smooth compared to 8-bit images.
❌ Calculate both: 2⁸ = 256 and 2²⁴ = 16,777,216. Divide: 16,777,216 ÷ 256 = 65,536×. The exponential nature of 2ⁿ means even small increases in bits cause huge jumps in colour count.
